def: n. f. Aug. ḍāṛhā , n. m. S. दाढिका beard. 1. Beard. 2. Hanging roots, as of the Baṛh. Baṛh kā ḍāṛhā . 1. Rūthe bābā ḍāṛhī hāth . Prov. The old man angry plucks his beard. (He is too feeble to hurt any one but himself.) 2. Kare ḍāṛhī-vālā. pakṛā jāë mūchhoṅ-vālā . Prov. The bearded man the crime commits, on spare moustache the justice sits. 3. Ḍāṛhī Khudā kā nūr hai . Prov. The beard is God's light. 4. Aur to maiṅ kyā kahūṅ ban āë ho laṅgār se! Dāṛhī muṅḍvāo, maiṅ bāz āī Khudā ke nūr se! Mah. Wom. Jān Sāhab. What sure can I say more—you've figged yourself out like an ape, Go, shave your beard! know I am cuts with such a god-like shape! 5. Pahle apnī hī ḍāṛhī kī āg bujhāī jātī hai . Prov. Every body puts out the flames in his own beard (before extinguishing the fire in another's). 6. Is ḍāṛhī kī sharam tumhāre hāth hai . This grey beard's shame is in your hands. ḍāṛhī phaṭkār'nā , v. a. To comb or smoothe the beard. Pathān laṛāī māreṅ, Banye ḍāṛhī phaṭkāreṅ . Prov. The Paṭhān does the fighting, the Bania strokes his beard. (Stroking one's beard is a sign of triumphant self-satisfaction). ḍāṛhī rakhnā , v. n. To keep a beard. ḍāṛhī kā ek ek bāl k., ḍāṛhī nochnā, yā khasoṭnā , Wom v. a. To pluck out one's beard. ḍāṛhī-munḍā , adj. Beard-shaven; beardless. peṭ meṅ ḍāṛhī h. , v. n. To be very sharp or knowing. Iske pet meṅ ḍāṛhī hai . Prov. He has a beard in his stomach. (An old head on young shoulders). khashkhāshī ḍāṛhī , n. f. A stubbly beard. khichṛī ḍāṛhī, kaṛ-baṛī ḍāṛhī , n. f. A grey beard. kūṅchī sī ḍāṛhī . n. f. A small thick beard. Dāṛhī hai, ki rāj kī kūṅchī? Phabtī . Chaff. Is this a beard, or a whitewasher's brush? būṛhī ḍāṛhī, sufaid ḍāṛhī , n. f. A white beard. Būṛhī dāṛhī le-kar to aisī bāteṅ na karo! Wom. ḍarhyā'lā , adj. Having a beard; bearded.

s. f. 1. A small carpet. 2. A dialect of the Persian language, the origin and peculiarities of which are variously defined by Persian lexicographers. Some alledge its distinctive mark to be when the form of the word is complete, without contraction; as bugo, ishkam, Darī; go and shikam, Pārsī. Others make it the dialect of a certain district, comprehending Balkh, Bukhārā, Badakhshān, Marū-Shāh-Jān, and Bāmiyān. The name is by some said to be derived from dara, a valley among hills; which corresponds with its origin in Badakhshān and the mountainous part of Kandhār. Others assert that it was spoken at the court of the monarchs of the Kaiānian dynasty, and derive the name from dar a gate or dargāh a court. ( Farh. Jahāng. Burh. Ḳāt̤. ). 3. (from دريدن) in compos. s. f. Rending, tearing.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَرِی (darī), from دَر (dar, “court”) + ـِی (-ī). By surface analysis, دَر (dar) + ـِی (-ī).

The new Daroga, Dharam Sing wants tribute of my topknot. ☞ The virtually absolute king within his jurisdiction was captivated by her hair, and he soon found a pretext for levying tribute, as Kanhaiya took tribute of milk and butter from the milkmaids. 2. An inspector of police; the prefect of a town or village. Dirogā chh/?/ṛ de morā piharvā, Tīn upaië chuprasyā ke deboṅ, pāṅch deboṅ kutvāl, Bālā jubanvā Phiraṅgyā ke deboṅ, piyā ko leboṅ chhuṛāë . E. Ḍ/?/mni. song. Daroga jī, I pray, release my husband dear! Rupees three for the guard, and five for you are here, To the Englishman my youth's ripe bloom to free my dear!

n. m. S. दर्श Sight; look; view. 1. Kāgā! nain nikās dūṅ, aur pī pās le jā! Pahle daras dikhāë-ke, pāchhe lījo khā! Dohā. I'll pluck my eyes out, crow, take take them to my love away! Let them first greet those eyes so dear, then feed on them you may! 2. Tū kyā daras merā dekhe, main hūṅ jāt kamīnā, Ghar ghar kā maiṅ pānī bhartī, pātī ṭakā mahīnā . Why do you fix your eyes on me, my caste you know is low, From house to house I pitchers fill, my monthly wage pice two. daras dikhānā , v. a. To show one's face to one.

def: s.m. The sea; the waters; a large river (the com. signification in India)
more: n. m. Pahlvī, daryāv ; Z. zrayaṅh ; Vedic S. jrayas . A river; (in P.) sea. 1. Nekī kar, aur daryā meṅ ḍāl! Prov. Do good and throw it in the river. (Expect not good in return). 2. Machhlī daryāo! Fishsellers' cry. River-fish! daryā-bar-āmad, daryā-barār , n. m. Alluvial land; alluvion; lands reclaimed from a river; increment of land. daryā-burd , n. Land carried away by the encroachments of a river; land cut away; diluvion. daryā-burd ho jānā , v. n. To be cut away by a river. daryā-burdī , n. f. Diluvion. daryā-pār , The other side of the river. daryā chaṛhnā , The rising of a river. daryā-dilī , n. f. lit . a heart as large as the sea. Great liberality; generosity. daryā-i-shor , n. m. The sea; ocean. daryā ko kūze meṅ band k . v. n. lit . to put the ocean into a goblet. 1. To say much in a few words; to be laconic, concise, or comprehensive. 2. To attempt impossibilities. palak daryā…

n. m. S. दशहरा from दश ten; and हृ seize. 1. The tenth of Jeṭh shukal paksh , the birthday of Gaṅga . ☞ Whoever bathes in the Ganges on this day, is purified from ten great sins. 2. The tenth of āsin shukal paksh , on which day, after the worship and religious ceremonies performed during nine nights ( navrātri ), images of Devī are thrown into the river. On this day Ram marched against Rāvan , for which reason it is called vijaë dasmī . This festival is celebrated with great pomp by Hindū princes, and the weapons and instruments of war are cleaned and worshipped; and, if war be intended, the campaign is then opened. 3. E. The Mah. marriage ceremony in which the bridegroom goes to the bride's house for ten days. dasahrā kharch , 1. The expenses attending the Dasehra festival. 2. A cess levied by a Zamindar on his tenants. It consists of rice, milk, ghee , etc.
more: s. m. 1. The tenth of Jeṭh shukl paksh̤, which is the birthday of Gangā. Whoever bathes in the Gangā on that day, is purified from ten sorts of sins. 2. The tenth of Āsin shukl paksh̤, on which, after the worship and religious ceremonies performed during nine nights ( नवरात्रि navarātri ) they throw the images of Devī into the river. On this day Rāma marched against Rāvaṇa; for which reason it is called vijai dasamī (see under بجي bijai ). The day is celebrated with great pomp by Hindū princes: the weapons and instruments of war are hallowed: and, if war be intended, the campaign is then opened.

ety: old P. daśta; Zend zaśta; S. hasta
def: s.m. Hand (syn. hāth); a cubit; — suit (of armour or clothes); anything complete (as a house with its offices); purging or loose stool, stool, purge, evacuation
more: n. m. Z. zasta ; S. हस्त 1. Hand; cubit. 2. Evacuation, usually purging or a loose stool; stool; purge. dast ānā , v. n. To have diarrhæa or loose bowels. dast-andāzī , n. f. Interference; encroachment; trespass; illegal exercise of authority. dast-andāzī k. , v. a. 1. ( dast-andāz h .,) To lay one's hands on; meddle; intermeddle; interfere. 2. To exercise illegal authority. dast-āvur ; Pop. dastāvar , adj. Purgative; cathartic. dastoṅ kī bīmārī ; H. peṭ-chalī , n. f. Diarrhæa.

s. m. 1. Hand, cubit. 2. Stool, purge, evacuation. دست افشان dast-afshān, Extending or moving hands in dancing, in relinquishing any thing, in anger or in denying. دست آموز dast-āmoz, adj. 1. Tame, familiarized, tractable. 2. Skilful, dexterous. دست انداز dast-andāz, 1. He who commences a business. 2. An oppressor, a plunderer. دست آويز dast-āwez, s. f. What a man takes with him as the means of promoting his suit; what a man gets into his hand and depends on; a small present which may be given into the hands of a person whose favour is sought, a signature, a note of hand, bond, title-deed, certificate, &c. دست بدست dast ba-dast, 1. Hand in hand, from hand to hand, quick, expeditious. 2. Ready-money purchase. دست برد dast-burd, s. m. Superiority, advantage, victory. دست بردارهونا dast-bardār honā, To leave off, desert, resign, desist, abstain, cease, relinquish, retire, decline. دست برسره…
subs: adj. & s.m. Extending or moving the hands in dancing, or in relinquishing anything, or in anger or excitement, or in denying; — one who moves the hands in dancing, &c.
adj. 'Accustomed to the hand,' tame, familiarized, gentle, tractable; — handy; skilful, dexterous
v.n. To have diarrhœa or loose bowels
adj. & s.m. Intermeddling, interfering; — one who interferes, &c.; one who commences a business; — an encroacher; an oppressor, a plunderer
s.f. Putting the hand (to, -meṅ, anything), meddling (with), interference; encroachment; oppression
(-meṅ), To put the hand (to), to meddle, &c.

s.f. 'That which ties or fastens the hands'; what a man takes with him as a means of promoting his suit; what one gets into his hand and depends on; a signature; a note of hand, bond, deed, title-deed, voucher, certificate, instrument, charter, &c.; — a small present to be given into the hands of a person whose favour is sought; — instance or example at hand
May good attend the hand (said by one who points out on his own body the seat of another's malady or pain)
adj. & adv. Hand in hand; from hand to hand; quick, expeditious; — s.m. ( = dast ba-dast muʻāmala), Ready-money purchase or transaction
To put up the hands in prayer
s.m. Pre-excellence, superiority, advantage, victory (at play, in war or law); encroachment, usurpation; plunder, rapine; misappropriation, embezzlement
(-se), To give up, abandon, relinquish, resign, to wash (one's) hands (of), to leave off, to let go, to desert; to abstain, desist (from), cease; to retire; to decline
s.f. Relinquishment, abandonment, renunciation; withdrawal (from); cessation
To be quite helpless, be unable to execute (one's) intention; to be in great grief, be distressed, weak, poor, or wretched
adv. With close or folded hands (in token of respect)
To salute (in the Eastern manner) by putting the hand to the head
adj. & adv. 'Hand to the hilt,' sword in hand, ready for battle
s.m. Cloth in which a small bundle is wrapped up
s.m. A string of pearls or precious stones worn (by women) on the wrist; — a gold or silver ornament worn on the back of the hand
To kiss the hand, to salute
s.f. Lightness of finger; stealth; — a civil war; a duel:-dast-ḵẖat̤, vulg. daskhat, daskat, s.m. 'Hand-writing'; signature; endorsement, or acceptance; — a manuscript, chirography
adj. Written with one's own hand; in manuscript; signed
adj. & s.m. 'Long-armed'; oppressive, extortionate; — an oppressor
s.f. Violence, oppression, extortion, exaction, excess
(-se, or -par?), To practice violence or oppression, to oppress, ill-treat, plunder, &c.

adj. Within (one's) reach or power; to be reached with the hand; able; come to hand, obtained; — s.f. Reach; ability, power, means; aid, assistance; facility; dexterity, skill; acquisition, attainment
v.n. To come to hand, to arrive; to be reached; to be obtained; to have in one's power, to be able
'Having healing in his hand,' an epithet of a physician who is successful in restoring his patients to health
s.m. lit. 'The hidden hand'; a charm or incantation by means of which a hidden hand supplies one's wants
s.m. A retailer, a hawker, a pedlar
s.f. Pedlary; the business of a pedlar
adj. Amputated in the hand; — s.m. A writer
adj. Wrought, manufactured; dexterous, expert, expeditious; — s.m. One who lives by the work of his hands, an artificer, handicraftsman, artizan, artist, a manufacturer
s.f. Manufacture, handicraft; art, trade; operation (in surgery); dexterity
adj. & s.m. Taken by the hand; led; — oppressed, weak; — a led horse; a guide (of the blind), a leader; a beggar; a captive
or dast-gah, s.f. Power, strength, ability, means; understanding; intellect; knowledge
adj. & s.m.? Going from hand to hand; hawked about; — money, &c. which is obtained on loan, a loan for a short period (on a verbal promise to pay); — anything hawked about for sale
(-ko), To lend (money, &c.) for a short period
adj. & s.m. Taken by the hand, helped; — one who is taken by the hand, a protégé
adj. & s.m. Taking by the hand; laying hands on; imprisoned; — a helper, assistant, patron, protector, a guardian-saint; — a prisoner
s.f. Assistance, aid, help; defence, support, protection, patronage
s.m.? The first money received for sale of merchandize in the morning, handsel (syn. bonī)
(ko), To feel a desire to ease nature; to have loose stools
s.m. lit. 'Hand-wiper'; a towel; napkin; handkerchief
dast-nigarān, adj. lit. 'Regarding the hand'; needy, wanting, in want
'To have the hand on the collar,' to engage in combat
adj. Attained, obtained; reached; attainable, procurable; — dast-yāb honā, v.n. To be attained, be acquired, be found, &c.; to attain (to), reach
s.m. A bracelet (of gold or silver).
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian دَسْت (dast)

def: signature (adjectival)
wety: From دستخط (dastxat) + ـی (-ī).

def: n. f. lit . hand-writing. 1. Signature; initials. 2. An endorsement or acceptance ( sakārā ). dast-khat k. , v. n. 1. To sign; to affix one's signature. 2. To endorse; endorse in blank.
wety: From Classical Persian دَسْتْخَطّ (dastxatt). Borrowed from Classical Persian دستخط (dastxat)
